source_metadata.title_raw: Anna Chouteau - A-19-052 - May 9,2019 - St. Helena

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 9, 2019

Anna Chouteau
1100 Paulson Court
Saint Helena, CA 94574

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-052

Dear Ms. Chouteau:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from taking part in decisions concerning the City of St. Helena's General Plan update ("update") where your spouse works for a business located outside of the City's jurisdiction but within one of the study areas of the update?

CONCLUSION

No. As explained below, the Act does not prohibit you from taking part in decisions concerning the update.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR

You are a member of the St. Helena City Council which is currently working on an update to the City's General Plan. The proposed update includes three study areas outside of the City limits. Your spouse works for a company located within one of these study areas. You stated the business includes wine production facilities, a tasting room and offices for employees. Other than minimal wine sales, your spouse's company does not do business within the City of St. Helena. The

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-052
Page No. 2

business is subject to the policies of the Napa County General Plan and related regulations
promulgated in the Napa County Code.

With respect to the update, a planning commission staff report provided:

As a comprehensive update, the 2040 General Plan provides a
framework for the City's physical, economic, and social development,
while also looking to preserve and protect environmental and
agricultural resources within the community. It addresses all geographic
areas within the City (and for the first time), begins to look at areas
beyond the City's boundary, but where City services are provided and
where external actions and development may affect City residents and
the provision of these services.

The draft update explains that the State of California encourages cities to look beyond their
borders when preparing a General Plan, as issues are not necessarily confined to political
boundaries. (See Gen. Plan Update, Chap. 1-8.) It also states the intent of including the three study
areas within the update is to document sensitive environmental resources and regulatory or capacity
constraints associated with those areas in the General Plan Environmental Impact Report ("EIR") in
order to inform decision-makers and community members about associated issues and
opportunities. (Ibid.)

According to the EIR, the planning area for the update includes three study areas in
unincorporated Napa County immediately adjacent to the City limits. (EIR at 3.2-12.) However,
because the City does not have jurisdiction outside of the City limits and Sphere of Influence
(SOI),² the policies and actions in the proposed update would neither apply to nor result in direct
impacts in the study areas. (Ibid.)³

You further state that there are no indications the City is attempting to acquire jurisdiction
over property included within the study areas. In this regard, you state the study area at issue is not
within the City's SOI, and there has been no discussion of pursuing annexation since you joined the
City Council.

2 The SOI represents the probable physical boundaries and service area of a local agency, determined by the
Napa County Local Agency Formation Commission (LAFCO) with input from the City. LAFCO establishes SOIs
throughout the county that promote the orderly expansion of cities and special districts to avoid the premature
conversion of agricultural and open-space lands and to ensure effective, efficient and economic provision of essential
public services, including public sewer and water, fire protection and emergency response, and police protection. (See
http://www.cityofsthelena.org/). St. Helena's Sphere of Influence and City limits are coterminous. (Ibid.)

3 The draft update states that it does not encourage any new development within the study areas (Gen. Plan
Update, Chap. 3.3-16) and does not include policies that pertain to the study areas (Id., Chap. 3.3-40). Furthermore, any
growth in the study areas between 2018 and 2040 buildout is the result of continued growth within unincorporated Napa
County and not directly as the result of change areas or land use designation modifications identified within the update.
(Id., Chap. 3.3-16.)

File No. A-19-052
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103 and
includes an interest in a source of income to the official including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) You would
have an interest in your spouse's business' as a source of income to the extent your community
property interest is $500 or more.⁵

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in
the decision – an official's interest is explicitly involved if the interest is a named party in, or the
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision at issue
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. Where an official's interest is not explicitly involved in
the decision, as here, Regulation 18701(b) provides that the effect is reasonably foreseeable if it
"can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical."

Regulation 18702.3 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an official's interest in a source of income. Regulation 18702.3(a)(4),
however, provides that materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, which sets forth
the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an official's business interest, if the
source of income is a business entity.

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's privately-
held business interest is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find that it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's effect will contribute to a change in the value of that
business.

According to the facts, the state encourages cities to consider property outside of their
borders when preparing a General Plan, which the City has done in its draft update. Nothing in the
EIR or the draft update itself suggests that it will have any financial impact on your spouse's wine
business, which does minimal wine sales within the City. In fact, the EIR states the policies and
actions in the proposed update would not apply to the study areas, and would not result in any direct
impacts in those areas. Furthermore, you state that there are no indications the City is attempting to
acquire jurisdiction over the property included within the study areas, which are not within the
City's SOI.

___________________________

⁴ Though your spouse's business is located outside the City of St. Helena limits, it does minimal business
within the jurisdiction and therefore qualifies as an economic interest. (See Section 82030.)

⁵ An official also has an interest in his or her personal finances. You have not described any effects on your
personal finances, therefore we do not further analyze this interest.

File No. A-19-052
Page No. 4

Therefore, based on the facts provided, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions relating to the General Plan update will have a material financial effect on your source of income interest in your spouse's employer. Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit you from taking part in those decisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: [signature]
Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JW: aja

concerning the update.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103 and 
includes an interest in a source of income to the official including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) You would 
have an interest in your spouse's business4 as a source of income to the extent your community 
property interest is $500 or more. 5 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in 
the decision - an official's interest is explicitly involved if the interest is a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision at issue 
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other 
entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. Where an official's interest is not explicitly involved in 
the decision, as here, Regulation 18701 (b) provides that the effect is reasonably foreseeable if it 
"can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." 
Regulation 18702.3 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect on an official's interest in a source of income. Regulation 18702.3(a)(4), 
however, provides that materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, which sets forth 
the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an official's business interest, if the 
source of income is a business entity. 
Regulation 18702.l(b) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's privately-
held business interest is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find that it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's effect will contribute to a change in the value of that 
business. 
According to the facts, the state encourages cities to consider property outside of their 
borders when preparing a General Plan, which the City has done in its draft update. Nothing in the 
EIR or the draft update itself suggests that it will have any financial impact on your spouse's wine 
business, which does minimal wine sales within the City. In fact, the EIR states the policies and

City's SOI. 
4 Though your spouse's business is located outside the City of St. Helena limits, it does minimal business 
within the jurisdiction and therefore qualifies as an economic interest. (See Section 82030.) 
5 An official also has an interest in his or her personal finances. You have not described any effects on your 
personal finances, therefore we do not further analyze this interest.  
Therefore, based on the facts provided, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions 
relating to the General Plan update will have a material financial effect on your source of income

concerning the City of St. Helena's General Plan update ("update") where your spouse works for a 
business located outside of the City's jurisdiction but within one of the study areas of the update?

CONCLUSION: No. As explained below, the Act does not prohibit you from taking part in decisions 
concerning the update.
